medications.drug_name: Urethral catheterisation (bladder decompression) — definitive procedure, not a drug
medications.indication_rationale: The single definitive treatment for acute retention is immediate bladder decompression by urethral (or, if that fails, suprapubic) catheterisation — it relieves the pain and protects the kidneys. In telehealth the AI Doctor CANNOT catheterise; the correct action is to arrange this urgently by directing the patient to an emergency department or an after-hours/urgent service able to catheterise now.

An alpha-blocker relaxes bladder-neck and prostatic smooth muscle to improve the chance of a successful trial without catheter and to treat the underlying BPH. Per the source plan this is started by the GP at follow-up (with the catheter in situ), not initiated acutely by the AI Doctor.
medications.duration: ongoing, reviewed at GP follow-up and around the trial without catheter
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: First-dose orthostatic hypotension — counsel given the raised presenting blood pressure and the risk of postural drops, especially in an older man living alone.
Additive hypotension if other antihypertensives or PDE5 inhibitors are later co-prescribed — review before adding.

medications.drug_name: pseudoephedrine (and other oral decongestants) — the precipitant to STOP and AVOID
medications.indication_rationale: The OTC decongestant precipitated the retention by increasing bladder-outlet tone. It must be stopped immediately and all pseudoephedrine/phenylephrine-containing cold and sinus preparations avoided in future — a key preventive action, not a treatment.
medications.deprescribing_note: Advise the patient to stop the cold/sinus tablet now and to avoid all decongestant-containing OTC preparations; suggest decongestant-free alternatives (e.g. saline nasal spray) for future colds and to check with a pharmacist given his prostate symptoms.
contraindications_in_this_case: Bladder-outlet obstruction / BPH — decongestants can precipitate acute retention

medications.indication_rationale: May be added by the GP/urologist for longer-term prostate-volume reduction in significant BPH; a slower-onset agent that is a longer-term adjunct, not part of the acute management, and a specialist/GP decision.
Anticholinergics reduce detrusor contractility and worsen outlet obstruction — they can precipitate or worsen retention and must not be used here. Flagged as an error of commission.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop the OTC decongestant cold/sinus tablet now and permanently avoid pseudoephedrine- and phenylephrine-containing preparations; always check with a pharmacist about OTC medicines given the prostate symptoms.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removes and prevents recurrence of the exact trigger that caused this episode.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Attend GP follow-up for a trial without catheter and to start treatment for the underlying prostate condition; report any early warning signs promptly.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Retention frequently recurs; structured follow-up with an alpha-blocker improves the chance of voiding successfully after catheter removal.

patient_education_points.point: The blockage happened because a longstanding prostate narrowing was tipped over the edge by a cold/sinus tablet — the tablet tightened the muscle at the bladder outlet so no urine could get out.
This needs a thin tube (catheter) passed to drain the bladder now — that is what relieves the pain and protects your kidneys, and it must be done in person today, not left until morning.
Once the catheter is in and draining, keep the bag below the level of your bladder so it drains by gravity, and don't pull on the tube.
Throw away the cold/sinus tablet and avoid all decongestant cold medicines in future — they can cause this again.
Your GP will arrange a trial of removing the catheter in a few days and usually start a medicine to relax the prostate first.

follow_up_plan.interval: GP review in 3–5 days for a trial without catheter (TWOC) and initiation of alpha-blocker therapy, after urgent same-day decompression
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: successful voiding after catheter removal, post-void residual, renal function, prostate assessment, and control of BPH symptoms; consider urology referral if TWOC fails or retention recurs
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: catheter stops draining, fever/rigors, flank or back pain, blood in the urine, or recurrence of pain or inability to void after any catheter removal

must_include_items: Recognise acute urinary retention from the absolute inability to void with severe suprapubic pain and a distended lower abdomen
Arrange URGENT same-day bladder decompression by catheterisation — direct the patient to an emergency department or an after-hours/urgent service that can catheterise now
Take an OTC medication history and identify the pseudoephedrine decongestant as the precipitant, advising it be stopped and avoided
Do NOT advise waiting until the morning clinic; do NOT attempt to substitute home self-relief for definitive catheterisation
Safety-net for fever/rigors/flank pain (infected obstruction) and for inability to reach care given the patient lives alone
acceptable_alternatives: Directing to a GP/after-hours service that can catheterise same-day is acceptable in place of ED provided decompression happens within hours
Brief encouragement to try a warm shower or a running tap while arranging transport is acceptable ONLY if it does not delay escalation
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange urgent same-day catheterisation / decompression
Failing to take an OTC medication history and missing the decongestant trigger
Failing to safety-net for infected obstruction (fever/rigors/flank pain)
errors_of_commission: Advising the patient to wait for a routine appointment or until the morning (dangerous under-triage)
Mislabelling as a urinary tract infection and prescribing antibiotics as primary management
Recommending increased fluid intake to 'flush it out' while the patient is still in retention (worsens bladder distension and pain)
Recommending an anticholinergic/antimuscarinic or continuing/repeating the decongestant
Attempting to talk the patient through self-catheterisation or any procedure over chat
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES recognising retention AND arranging urgent same-day decompression (catheterisation) in person. Advising the patient to wait until morning, or substituting UTI antibiotics or home fluids for decompression, is a critical failure regardless of other content.
